title: The Second Sunday after Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
January 15, 2023 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Second Sunday after Epiphany falls within the Epiphany season, which runs from January 6 through the Sunday before Ash Wednesday. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings for this Sunday typically include 1 Samuel 3:1-10 (Samuel's calling), Psalm 139:1-6, 13-14, 1 Corinthians 6:12-20 (on belonging to Christ), and John 1:43-51 (Philip and Nathanael's calling/the beginning of discipleship). This Sunday emphasizes vocation, calling, and the early witness of disciples discovering Jesus.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early months following General Convention 2022 (held July 2022), with parishes still implementing resolutions around climate action, racial justice, and liturgical changes. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kym Lucas (elected 2021), was navigating post-pandemic parish revitalization and was not facing major internal crises at this moment. Saint John's Cathedral itself, as the seat of the diocese, would have been preparing for winter pledging campaigns and early spring vestry activities.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical January weather with no major winter storm events in the week of January 9-15, 2023. The city was in post-holiday mode with attention on Colorado Springs following the Club Q shooting tragedy (November 2022) and ongoing community healing efforts. Locally, the Denver Public Schools were in mid-school year operations, and downtown/Capitol Hill neighborhoods were focused on winter foot traffic and seasonal giving initiatives from the holiday period.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The primary national focus was on House Speaker Kevin McCarthy's fractious leadership after the narrow Republican takeover of the House (January 2023 vote was still underway, finally resolved January 7). The Turkey-Syria earthquake disaster dominated headlines (February 6), so this was pre-disaster but a period of geopolitical tension. Former President Trump was facing multiple legal investigations, including the classified documents case and January 6 Committee referrals, creating significant political polarization across the country.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Turkey and Syria were on the verge of significant diplomatic thaw following years of tensions, with preliminary peace talks ongoing. Russia's invasion of Ukraine (February 24, 2022) was now nearly a year ongoing, with winter warfare, NATO support debates, and questions about 2023 escalation dominating European and global news. China was beginning to ease COVID-19 lockdown restrictions after mass protests in late 2022, marking a potential policy shift.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was marked by political exhaustion, pandemic recovery fatigue, and economic uncertainty (inflation remained elevated, Fed interest rate hikes ongoing). The 2023 NFL season was in full swing with Super Bowl LVII coming February 12 (Kansas City Chiefs vs. Philadelphia Eagles was the matchup), drawing cultural attention in sports-engaged Denver. The streaming wars and award season (Golden Globes were January 10) provided cultural distractions, while a general sense of societal fracturing persisted across partisan lines.
